text: Hello everyone, this is Dr. Madhya Leba. This is the second patient that was going to show up to my live connect surgery. Unfortunately, this patient cancelled. This is the subsequent video.

This patient has a symptomatic incisional ventral hernia with incarceration of bowel and omentum. She's had a prior C-section which is likely the cause of her problems. This can be quite difficult as the inferior portion of the lower abdominal area is very weak and the fascia in this area can be difficult to deal with. Most times there's actually no fascia, there's just muscle. As per my routine, I like to go in triptomally, evaluate the hernia on this side, what type of repair I'm going to do. In this case, I wasn't sure if I was going to be able to do an ETEP or if I had to do a formal TAR or IPOM. Once I found what I was dealing with, I felt that I could do an ETEP. I first did a lateral dock on the left side, did a crossover, and then repositioned my ports for a top-down approach.

The surgeon performs a contralateral dissection around the hernia site, ensuring no bowel is present within the hernia sac. The dissection reveals attenuated muscle and minimal fascia fibers, requiring careful reconstruction of the posterior components and closure of the anterior components.

The surgeon mobilizes the fragile peritoneum along the space of Retzius, performing bilateral lower abdominal transversus abdominis releases (TAR) to achieve closure and provide coverage for the suprapubic defect.

The surgeon anchors the sine core mesh to the pubis using several probing sutures to maintain integrity in the region due to the unhealthy tissue and significant defect. Drains are placed to manage fluid accumulation, and fibrin glue is applied to seal the space after insufflating the intra-abdominal region.

The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity post-repair, confirming no mesh interactions, exposure, or complications. There are no visible bowel injuries or peritoneal tears. The fibrin glue is settling, and drains are in place to suction and collapse the space. A laparoscopic tap block is performed using dexbarilla and marcaine.
